text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultrasound to locate a radiopaque object in the duodenum. The duodenotomy is created based on ultrasound guidance, revealing the object despite technical difficulties with TilePro visualization.

The surgeon places a Maryland dissector inside the first part of the anterior wall of the duodenum, attempting to locate the radiopaque object amidst significant edema. Two laparoscopic instruments are used for tactile feedback, but locating the object remains challenging. The duodenotomy is extended transversely, revealing the area injected with ICG, as the object is oriented vertically.

The surgeon removes the falciform ligament, planning to use it as a flap for repair. Ultrasound is repeated to locate the radiopaque foreign body, identified as a chicken bone, on the posterior wall of the duodenum. An incision is made on the mucosa of the posterior wall to access the object.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to close the mucosal defect on the posterior wall of the duodenum. A 2-0 silk suture is placed in the middle to align the duodenum for closure. The closure is reinforced with a third layer of silk sutures, securing the falciform flap. Endoscopy is attempted but initially difficult due to the reinforced sutures, requiring removal of some sutures to pass the scope successfully.
